votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by filtering the list for elements of type int and comparing the result to the expected list.
The Python code correctly implements the filtering logic of the Coq relation by keeping only elements of type int, which matches the behavior of the fir_cons_int and fir_cons_nonint cases.
The Python code correctly implements the filtering logic of the Coq relation by selecting only integer elements from the input list.

The Coq spec defines filter_integers_rel which filters a list keeping only elements that satisfy IsInt (are integers), preserving order. The Python code filters values keeping only those where type(v) is int, then checks equality with res. Both extract exactly the integer elements from the input list in order. The Coq IsInt predicate corresponds to Python's type(v) is int check - both identify which elements are integers and collect them. The Python implementation correctly produces the same concrete results as the Coq specification for any input list.

The Coq spec defines filter_integers_rel which filters a list keeping only elements that satisfy IsInt (i.e., are integers) in order. The Python code uses a list comprehension to filter values keeping only those where type(v) is int, then checks equality with res. Both compute the same result: the subsequence of integer elements from the input list. The Coq relation builds the filtered list inductively (keeping integers, skipping non-integers), and the Python list comprehension does the same filtering. For concrete inputs, both will produce/check the same filtered list of integers.

The Coq spec defines filter_integers_rel which keeps elements that satisfy IsInt (are integers) and drops those that don't. The Python code filters values keeping only those where type(v) is int, then checks if this equals res. Both compute the same filtering operation on concrete inputs: extract integers from the input list and compare to res. The relational Coq definition and the functional Python definition produce the same concrete results for any given list of values.

Both produce the list of integers from the input, in order; Python keeps elements whose type is int and returns them unchanged, matching the Coq relation when IsInt relates a value to its unique integer.

Both define the result as the list of integers from the input, in order. The Python code filters values of type int and compares to res, matching the Coq relation that includes exactly those v for which IsInt v n holds and outputs n.
It checks that res equals the subsequence of integer elements from values in order, which matches the Coq relation: include n when IsInt v n holds and skip otherwise, yielding a unique result.
